name: Honda CR-V
engine: 1.5L turbo I4 (L15B7) — 2.4L naturally-aspirated I4 (K24W9) on the 2017 LX
reliability: 8/10 — among America's best-selling SUVs, so its raw NHTSA complaint count is large mostly by sales volume. The genuinely recurring, documented items are narrow: the early 1.5T's cold-climate oil dilution, which Honda addressed with a service bulletin and a powertrain-warranty extension, and 2017–2018 A/C condenser cracks, for which Honda extended coverage. Mechanically it's Honda-durable and cheap to keep.
cost_to_own: Insurance: Low (family SUV, low theft/claim profile) · Maintenance ~$400/yr · Strong resale — quietly where the CR-V wins
popular_mods: All-weather floor liners (WeatherTech / Husky) — the real 'first mod' for a daily SUV
Roof crossbars + cargo box (Yakima / Thule) for the family-hauler missions
A second set of wheels with dedicated winter tires — the single best 'performance' upgrade here
Trailer hitch + bike or cargo rack (rated ~1,500 lbs braked)
Honest take: the CR-V is an appliance, not a mod platform — money is better spent on good tires and on-time maintenance than on power
oil_interval: Per the Maintenance Minder (~7,500 miles) — don't stretch it on the 1.5T

generations.5th Gen (2017–2022): The volume CR-V most shoppers cross-shop against the RAV4. 1.5L turbo (190 hp) from EX on up; the 2017 LX kept the older 2.4L naturally-aspirated four. CVT only. Roomy, efficient, and genuinely pleasant to live with, with Honda Sensing driver-assist standard on most trims.
generations.Hybrid added (2020+): A two-motor CR-V Hybrid joined the lineup for 2020 (~38+ mpg city) for buyers who want the efficiency without plugging in.
generations.Replaced by the 6th Gen (2023+): The 2023 redesign grew the CR-V and leaned harder into the hybrid, which makes a clean 2019–2022 1.5T the value sweet spot on the used market.

common_issues: 1.5T oil dilution: fuel can wash into the oil during short, cold-weather trips — watch for a rising oil level and a fuel smell, worst on early 2017–2018 cars before Honda's fix
A/C condenser failures on 2017–2018 (stress cracks / debris) — a common, well-documented CR-V and Civic-era complaint
Infotainment lag and the deleted volume knob on pre-refresh units (the knob returned in the 2020 update)
More road and wind noise than some rivals — a comfort gripe, not a mechanical one
maintenance_tips: Change the oil on time and check the level between changes if you do short cold-weather hops — that's the 1.5T's one real quirk
Cold-climate owners: get the dealer software update and let the engine warm up properly before short trips
Service the CVT fluid with genuine Honda HCF-2 around 30–40k miles — CVTs go the distance when you actually service them
Budget for the A/C condenser on an early car if it hasn't already been replaced
Beyond that it's cabin filters, brakes, and tire rotations — this is a cheap car to keep
